That flooding situation in [NCR] really brought me trauma and possibly my anxiety back. I am in no place to belittle others’ experiences esp to those who have had it harder than mine but i would like to share what happened to me. I have been a flood victim twice, the first time around was the hardest and the second time around was manageable and we still had to move houses for both I speak with experience and fear here that even the university I go to isn’t resilient with floods but now all i feel is this constant feae of having ti move out because there is nothing to salvage anymore. Not that the campus was entirely ruined but I don’t feel safe anymore and what if things get worse? I was in a car on the way home from campus with my dad when suddenly we couldn’t really see anything and the car was difficult to move. I felt instant panic because it feels like before. By that time the water was rising fast the car displayed all kinds of lights on the dashboard and thats when I knew this isn’t going to end well. My dad at least kept himself together to park a little more off the water but the water still kept rising, we had to get out. As I stood there outside of our car I just had to make sure this car doesn’t drown, it was the only car my family could save up after such a while. Rather than the panic in myself, I saw more my dad’s sadness of us going through this again, and this time in an unexpected moment of flloding for us. We were just on the way home. While we were panic calling my family at home, all my dad and I could do was watch our car go above wheel level in the flood. We were around Shakey’s at the time. But after everything has settled and the flood subsided to move through again, my dad instantly told me not to go to school for a couple of days. Was it fear? was it reflection? or was it his protection? I for sure feel it is out of fear. But I am a senior and I don’t plan on leaving school so late now. What I’m trying to say is, can [NCR] do something about this? surely out of the infrastructure we build around campus can improve drainage systems and even campus buildings to be more rain resilient because why was [REDACTED_NAME] 2nd floor having water in it?? I know ig must be a privilege to ask of this considering people who cannot ask for more accountability esp for their livelihoods, but what about [NCR]? what about the tuition we pay every semester adnd what about the admin who “listens” to the student body and workforce? we are all under the same roof here, its as if its our second home, not that I don’t want to panic further and escalating things to the worst case scenario but what if a thunderstorm happens like this again? How can [NCR] ensure the safety of its community? not just college students but even grade school and JHS. I hope you guys can understand where I’m coming from and I don’t want to put all blame in [NCR] but just want to be assured of our safety because I have my anxieties going to classes at this time of rainy season.

I don't have it in me to write anymore...but if I don't write then I might just lose my will to live. I’m a transwoman who was falsely accused of sexual harassment for over a year now. When I sit down to write this, that numbing mental haze only serves to build up over time. Not build a tolerance to it. This is the difficulty of writing about brain damage. A few months later, I was talking to my psychiatrist about how when you have trauma and abuse, everything you make is about trauma and abuse. This is about disposability from a transfeminine perspective. I was pretty new to the trans community in [NCR], and instead of finding safety I found something worse than anything I’d experienced before. What many TME students don’t know is that trans fems are often impoverished, disabled, and/or from traumatic backgrounds. I’ve known plenty of trans spaces that talk about binding but when I talk about wanting sources for “tucking” suddenly I’m told it’s too ‘sexual’. You are not actually growing if you refuse to consider the idea that other queer people can reinforce and benefit from transmisogyny on smaller levels too. Worse, the person who made these rumors about me was a half-white transman. It’s always a double standard for us. If we ever fuck up, we are treated as less-than-human. If we ever fuck up, we are treated on a double standard. Young queer 20-something year olds come into these spaces and encounter concepts for the first time and many of us have no idea what is “normal” or not. I was hoping we would be offered forgiveness and grace and be told when we screw up. I was wrong. I always double up laughing while everyone in my class calls me a ‘sexual harasser.’ Meanwhile I can’t even go in a dress to a public jeep without getting harassed by men. I’m not safe here. And, I was deemed unsafe by people who were supposed to be my community. I have nowhere else to go. Trans fems are especially vulnerable to this kind of abuse for these following reasons. Worse, I never got to even know what the sexual harassment allegations were. I was just told that people had to leave me. Every single person in my life who I thought was safe ended up killing me this way. And they believed my abuser instead. I spent the last year not making connections or gaining valuable socialization that I had already missed as a teen, but being exploited and gaslit into social isolation. I was scared away from queer/trans/feminist spaces/talks, and everything else that people like to pretend is available for trans fems. Instead of solidarity from other marginalized people in my field, I was attacked. People started out by calling me a sexual harasser, then it escalated to me being an assaulter, then next pedophile. predator and rapist. Every trans dog whistle imaginable. I did everything I could to de-escalate it while they were constantly escalating it. All the meanwhile, I was trying not to have a panic attack everyday while constantly being exposed to violence. Constantly being told to be ‘accountable’ for something I cannot be accountable for. Worse, every single one of my peers was fine with me being abused because they saw it as justified. The Gender Hub didn't help... The goal was clear: to keep attacking me until I broke—until every imaginable accusation had been thrown my way. They framed me as a vulnerable, isolated trans girl—someone who could barely leave her room most days—as some kind of threat, as someone with power I never had. That power, in truth, belonged to them: people deeply embedded in queer and political spaces, backed by years of political clout and social influence. There was no warning. No opportunity for resolution. I had sensed the tension and directly asked for mediation several times. Every time, they refused. It’s easiest if you’re never given the chance to speak up. When I privately shared that I was being abused, it was twisted and used against me—my words reworked to fit a false timeline that justified their cruelty. Once my work started gaining visibility, that quiet exclusion turned into a public crusade. Those rumors balled into a harassment campaign against me. The accusations had to be loud and severe—fabricated crimes tailored to stir enough disgust to drive me out completely. That’s the danger of being in the token system: you get spotlighted just enough to be publicly destroyed. Escaping from abuse is the most certain way to become painted as an abuser. Being mobbed for months was emotional abuse. For a long time, I had to re-learn unlearning sexual shame. I was shamed into never talking about sex or trans sex ever again for fear of someone using that against me. Of course, you can’t expel someone anymore by calling them a whore or a slut. You have to tie it down to 21st century PC language in order to mask what you’ve really been doing - expelling a trans girl because you feel she has ‘invaded your space’. I don’t know what else to do...I lost all my friends. I’m afraid of the queer community here now. And my only proof of this happening was that I went to the doctor for proof of the PTSD this caused me. I wasn’t really living. I’ve just been surviving. By the end of the day my neck and left arm are aching from nervous tics. My hair fell out in handfuls. The worst part was not being able to talk about this. Everyone had demonstrated to me that they did not care about transfems being abused. Anything about ‘woman’s spaces’ was a hoax. When you’re not an out t-girl or someone who doesn’t look feminine enough by cis people, and you come asking for help that there are rumors about you being a ‘sexual harasser’, what is the first thing they do? They don’t believe you and they leave without a word. And the cycle repeats. Don’t ever get your hopes up about making friends! Remember you don’t have a life. It’s owned by another man who wants to control you. I just want to feel happy again. I want to feel like I’m alive again. I feel numb.

This is the "priest-in-training" speaking from that one Freedom Wall submission. God truly works in mysterious ways; your true calling will find you in some form or way. I'd like share my story: I was a very insecure guy growing up, especially in college, when I spectated how people lived their lives to the fullest. I didn't look my best and I had such a hard time adjusting to school as a scholar with a lot of family issues. Meeting such a beautiful and intelligent woman stirred something in me and it terrified me. I was so afraid of hurting her and getting hurt, alongside my general insecurity. I buried myself in my studies and let myself get swept away by vocational work. I was (and still am) passionate in theology, philosophy, and working with the church, but I was definitely running away from a life I was afraid to lead. If I listened to my heart, I would have loved passionately, but loving meant risking hurt. As a naive boy, I thought "[REDACTED_NAME], hindi ba?" so I decided to throw myself into the path of being a priest. Not very wise of me. I realized that I was hurting myself by running away from the truth and just letting myself be a mere voyeur of the woman who had my heart. My heart grew weary and I was ready to embrace the consequences of my cowardice until something happened---we met again (in rather unfortunate circumstances, may mama [REDACTED_NAME]'s soul rest in peace). We got coffee and it felt like talking to a best friend, like we just picked up where we left off. She's always been so beautiful, but all the more now. I can't believe I've been using God as an excuse not to live my life. How foolish and cowardly of me! Something just clicked inside me. This is where I'm meant to be. Fast forward, I left the path to priesthood while still pursuing my passions in the academe. I felt a lot of embarrassment but everyone was so supportive of me, albeit a lot of discernment. I'm finally following my heart, as God intended for everyone to do so. We're going strong and I have never felt His presence this much before. Follow your heart and it'll all be worth it; so go on that coffee date you've always been scared of. Talk to them again, if your heart says so. It might just change your life. P.S. Yes, we watched [REDACTED_NAME] together and we cried together. It's a great show and we're happy to say that we got a different ending. She joked about being scared of saying 'I love you' because she was so scared that I might say 'it'll pass'. I laughed and simply said 'Well, I do love you and I don't think it'll pass'. I am but a man who is so fortunate to experience God's love in the most beautiful woman's arms, among the many other wonderful people and things in my life where He can be felt. Thank you for reading about our story. I apologize for not being too poetic in this submission since I wanted to be as candid as possible. :)

To admins: This is my first time posting here, and I know there’s a chance my post might not make it through (since there’s probably a lot to review, huhu). But admins, I truly hope you approve this—I just want my words to reach someone. [REDACTED_NAME], It’s been more than two years since we met—since I saw you as more than just a friend. Somewhere between shared laughter and quiet conversations, my heart betrayed me. I fell for someone who never once looked at me the way I looked at them. At first, I thought I could admire you from a distance, keeping my feelings buried. But no matter how I tried to bury them, they surfaced—loud, clear, impossible to ignore. The saddest part? You knew—but it never meant enough to matter. Not to acknowledge it beyond fleeting words, not to spare me even a moment of consideration. You simply carried on, unaffected, while I was left to wrestle with emotions that refused to fade. Months later, I confessed. You said you were flattered—because it was me. But you’d never date a friend. I should find someone else. And I’ve spent so long wondering what any of that truly meant. Were those words meant to comfort me? To push me away? Or were they just a gentle way of telling me I never stood a chance? But still, we carried on as if nothing had changed. And I played along, pretending I hadn’t spilled my heart at your feet. Because if it meant keeping you close, a little more pain was a price I was willing to pay. Then one day, I heard you liked someone. Not your first love, the one whose name still lingered in your stories. Not the ex you once spoke of with longing. But someone new. Someone who would never be me. That’s when I knew—I had to save myself from you. My friends called me foolish, said I was worse than a martyr, and I never argued. I carried my unrequited love like a quiet burden, until the moment I learned you had feelings for someone else. That’s when I realized—I deserve better. And I had to choose. Because I cannot be a genuine friend to you. Because I cannot sit there and listen as you tell me about her. It’s either I stay—your friend who loves you in silence—or I walk away, a stranger, so I can finally let you go. After a year, I truly believed I had moved on, even while still being your friend. I liked other guys, found some attractive, and felt free from the weight of you. For the first time, I felt happy—because I thought I finally had. But it was never as simple as I made it seem. If I had really let you go, then why do these words still find their way out of me? Why does the question still linger, unspoken yet relentless—why was I never the one you wanted? Every song, every movie, every love story I come across—it’s never you and me that I picture. It’s always you and her, or the ones who came before, or the ones who might come after. Because I never once believed we were a possibility. I never let myself dream of an "us," never imagined the lyrics of a love song belonging to our story, never saw myself in the place of the girls you’ve liked. Instead, I watched from the outside, memorizing the way your eyes lit up when you spoke about someone else, tracing the outline of a love that never had space for me. So when I hear a song about longing, about love slipping through fingers, about someone being just a little too late—I don’t hear it as ours. I hear it as yours, sung for someone else, while I stay in the background, listening.��But maybe that’s what sets this feeling apart. It was never about wanting to be the one you chose; it was simply about you. Because if this was just another fleeting infatuation, it would have faded like all the rest. But it didn’t. No matter how hard I tried, no matter who else caught my eye, you remained. Maybe love isn’t loud or grand, but quiet—stubborn, unshakable, lingering when it shouldn’t. Maybe that’s what makes it hurt so much. It doesn’t simply disappear; it settles deep, weaving itself into the spaces between memories, the pauses between words, the moments I thought I had left behind. It lingers in the jokes we shared, in the way you said my name, in the places we always stood side by side. It’s in the songs that remind me of laughter that wasn’t quite ours to keep, in the conversations that never meant as much to you as they did to me. And maybe that’s why moving on has never been easy—because how do you let go of something that never even had the chance to begin? Maybe that’s the curse of unrequited love. It’s not just about loving without being loved back. It’s about loving while being seen as nothing more than a friend. It’s watching the person you cherish look past you, never even pausing to wonder if you could be something more. And the cruelest part isn’t that you didn’t love me—it’s that you never even considered it. Maybe I’m one of those unfortunate souls doomed to love only once in their lifetime—to give their heart away without ever getting it back. Some people fall in and out of love with ease, their hearts resilient, always ready for a new beginning. But what if mine isn’t like that? What if I’ve already given away the only love I had to offer? What if no matter how many years pass, no matter how many new faces I meet, a part of me will always belong to you? It’s a cruel thought, really—to think that my heart has chosen you, and no matter how distant or unattainable you’ve always been, it refuses to choose again. And maybe that’s the truth I’ve been avoiding—that a part of me will always be waiting, unwillingly, unknowingly. Not because I want to, not because I believe you will ever see me the way I see you, but because my heart doesn’t know how to do anything else. I will wait in the quiet, not with hope, but with resignation—because it’s you or nothing. Maybe that’s just the way my heart was built—to love once, to love deeply, and to never truly let go, no matter how much I wish it would. Because love like this doesn’t need a reason. It doesn’t ask for permission. It simply stays. But maybe, in some way, I was loved too. Not in the way I wanted—not with stolen glances, whispered confessions, or the certainty of belonging to one another—but in the way you knew how. You never loved me romantically, but you let me in. You let me stay. And maybe that’s a kind of love too. Maybe love isn’t always about being chosen in the way you long for. Maybe it’s in the way you listened when I rambled about the smallest things, in the way you remembered the details I never expected you to. Maybe it was in the quiet moments, in the unspoken trust, in the certainty that, even if I was never the one for you, I was still someone who mattered. And for that, I am grateful. Because even though a part of me ached for something more, I know what we have is real. You may have never seen me as anything beyond a friend, but you cared for me in the way that you could. And maybe that’s enough. Maybe love doesn’t have to be requited to be meaningful. Maybe the way you loved me—as a friend, as someone you valued—was enough for me to carry forward, even as I learned to leave the rest behind. Maybe, in the end, love is love, no matter the form it takes. And even if I was never the one you wanted, I can still be thankful for the way you made me feel seen, for the way you let me be a part of your life, for the way you loved me—not as I wished, but in the only way you knew how. And yet, despite everything, I know a part of me will still wait. For what, I can’t say. Not for a promise, not for a change of heart—but for something I can’t quite name. Maybe I’m waiting for the day this feeling softens into something quieter, something that no longer weighs so heavily on me. Maybe I’m waiting to prove to myself that I can love again. Or maybe, I’m simply waiting for the moment I no longer feel like I’m waiting at all. And now, more than two years later, I find myself here—not just carrying the weight of what was never mine, but learning, slowly, to set it down. Somewhere between moving forward and holding on, I’m beginning to realize that my heart, no matter how stubborn, will one day stop waiting. Maybe I’ll never stop loving you, but I’ll learn how to live through it—how to let this love exist without needing you by my side. And maybe that’s enough. Yours, in a way you’ll never know [REDACTED_NAME]

since a lot of profs2pick reviews are qualitative and depend on what you want to get out of the class, here's a quick and simple profs alignment chart! - quadrant 1: harsh, learn a lot. many legendary profs are in this quadrant. they will change your life for the better as long as you're willing to risk changing your qpi for the worse. - quadrant 2: kind, learn a lot. all around amazing profs. these are the ones who everyone wants to get during enlistment. - quadrant 3: kind, learn nothing. chill but don't teach. the reqs are easy and the workload is light, but you aren't going to come out of the class with the learning you paid for - quadrant 4: harsh, learn nothing. terror profs. self explanatory
